workout.title: Chest Day - BODYBUILD
workout.description: Chest Day workout focusing on bodybuild training.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.
instructions: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.

Lie supine on the bench with your head, upper back and glutes in contact with the bench, feet flat on the floor and a small natural arch in the lower back; retract and depress your shoulder blades to create a stable chest platform.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists should be stacked over the elbows and forearms vertical when the bar is over the chest.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar out over the chest while keeping the scapulae stable; lock the elbows briefly before starting the set.
Inhale and lower the bar in a controlled manner to the mid-chest/sternum (around nipple level), keeping the elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso to protect the shoulders.
Allow the bar to touch or come very close to the chest without bouncing; maintain tension in the pectorals and keep the feet and glutes braced.
Exhale and press the bar upward explosively while keeping the bar path slightly arced toward the hips until the arms are fully extended but not hyperextended.
Pause briefly at the top with the shoulders stable, then begin the next rep. Maintain consistent form and breathing throughout the set.
Use a spotter or safety pins in the rack for heavy sets, and avoid flaring the elbows excessively or letting the lower back lift off the bench.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a compound pressing movement performed lying supine on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and anterior delts. It involves lowering a loaded barbell to the chest and pressing it back up to full arm extension with controlled technique.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and place a pair of dumbbells nearby.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and place a pair of dumbbells nearby.
Sit on the bench with the dumbbells resting on your thighs, feet flat on the floor for stability.
Use your legs to help lift the dumbbells as you lie back so the weights are beside your upper chest; hold them with a neutral or slightly supinated grip.
Retract your shoulder blades and press your upper back into the bench so your chest is proud (maintain a stable supine position on the incline).
Plant your feet and brace your core to avoid excessive arching of the spine.
Press the dumbbells upward and slightly together in a controlled path until your arms are almost fully extended (do not lock the elbows).
Exhale as you press up, pause briefly at the top with control, then inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the start position at the upper chest level.
Keep the elbows at a moderate angle (not flared excessively) and the wrists neutral throughout the movement.
Repeat for desired repetitions and use a spotter for heavy sets or if you cannot safely return the weights to your thighs/stand them up.
exercises.description: The dumbbell incline bench press is performed lying supine on an incline bench and pressing dumbbells upward to emphasize the upper portion of the chest. It builds strength and mass in the upper pectorals while also recruiting the front delts and triceps.

exercises.name: Cable Bent Over Single Arm Crossover
exercises.notes: Set a single handle on a cable pulley at about shoulder to chest height and select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Set a single handle on a cable pulley at about shoulder to chest height and select an appropriate weight.

Stand to the side of the pulley with a staggered or shoulder-width stance and hinge forward at the hips until your torso is roughly 30–45° from vertical (slight bend in knees); maintain a neutral spine and brace your abs.
Grab the handle with the hand farthest from the machine, arm extended out to the side and palm facing down or slightly inward; allow a soft bend in the elbow.

Keep your shoulder down and stable, then exhale and pull the handle in a horizontal arc across your body toward the center of your chest, leading with the elbow and squeezing the pectorals at the end range.
Pause briefly with the chest contracted, keeping the torso still (avoid rotating the ribs or shoulders), then inhale and slowly reverse the motion to return the hand to the start under control.
Perform the desired reps, then switch sides and repeat. Maintain wrist neutrality and controlled tempo throughout.
exercises.description: A single-arm cable movement performed from a bent-over stance that sweeps the working arm across the body to load and compress the chest fibers, emphasizing the inner and upper pectoral region while the torso is stabilized.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

workout.description: Chest Day workout focusing on bodybuild training.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
Grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width, wrists stacked over elbows and thumbs wrapped around the bar.
Retract and depress the shoulder blades (pinch the shoulder blades together) to create a stable chest platform.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar over the chest so it is balanced and steady.
Inhale, lower the bar under control to the mid-chest (sternum) keeping elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso.
Pause briefly with the bar touching or just above the chest while maintaining tightness through the core and legs.
Exhale and drive the bar upward by extending the elbows, pressing the bar back to the start position while keeping wrists neutral.
Avoid excessive elbow flare or bouncing the bar off the chest; maintain a controlled tempo and keep feet planted.
After the set, carefully re-rack the bar by guiding it back onto the bench supports before releasing your grip.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a supine pressing exercise performed on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also working the triceps and anterior delts. The lifter lowers a barbell to the chest and presses it back to full arm extension under control.

exercises.name: Barbell Incline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set an adjustable bench to a 30-45° incline and position it inside a power rack or with a spotter and place the barbell on the rack at a height you can reach while seated.
instructions: Set an adjustable bench to a 30-45° incline and position it inside a power rack or with a spotter and place the barbell on the rack at a height you can reach while seated.
Sit with your back and head firmly against the bench, feet flat on the floor, and brace your core (tighten abs and glutes) to maintain a stable, slight arch in the lower back.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists stacked over elbows and shoulders pulled down and back.
Unrack the bar by straightening your arms and moving the bar directly above your upper chest/shoulder line; have a spotter if needed.
Inhale, bend the elbows, and lower the bar under control to the upper chest/clavicle area (touch point just below the top of the sternum) keeping elbows at roughly 30–60° from the torso.
Pause briefly at the bottom without bouncing, ensuring the forearms remain vertical and the core stays braced.
Exhale and press the bar back up in a slightly backward arc until the arms are fully extended but not locked aggressively at the elbows.
Repeat for the desired repetitions, then carefully re-rack the bar on the uprights with control or have the spotter assist.
exercises.description: The barbell incline bench press is a compound pressing movement performed on an inclined bench that emphasizes the upper portion of the pectorals while also engaging the anterior delts and triceps. It is done from a supine position on an incline bench, pressing a barbell vertically from chest to full arm extension.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Fly
exercises.notes: Step 1: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet planted on the floor and a neutral spine; hold a dumbbell in each hand above your chest with palms facing each other and elbows slightly bent.
instructions: Step 1: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet planted on the floor and a neutral spine; hold a dumbbell in each hand above your chest with palms facing each other and elbows slightly bent.
Step 2: Brace your core and gently retract and depress your shoulder blades to stabilize the shoulder girdle throughout the movement.

Step 3: Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells out to the sides in a wide arc, keeping the same slight bend in your elbows and stopping when your upper arms are about parallel to the floor or you feel a comfortable stretch in the chest (avoid dropping below bench level).

Step 4: Pause briefly at the bottom without relaxing the shoulders, then exhale and drive the dumbbells back up in the same arc by contracting the pectorals until the weights meet or are close together over your sternum.
Step 5: Maintain controlled tempo (no bouncing), keep elbows fixed at the same angle, and avoid locking the arms at the top; repeat for desired reps.
exercises.description: Lying flat on a bench while holding dumbbells, the dumbbell fly opens the arms in a wide arc and brings them together to isolate and stretch the chest (pectorals). Movement emphasizes a controlled, arcing motion with a slight bend in the elbows to protect the shoulder joint.

exercises.name: Lever Incline Fly
exercises.notes: Adjust the machine so the back pad is set to an incline (approximately 30–45°) and select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Adjust the machine so the back pad is set to an incline (approximately 30–45°) and select an appropriate weight.
Sit with your back pressed firmly against the pad, feet flat on the floor, chest lifted and shoulders down; grasp the handles with a neutral grip and arms out to the sides at roughly chest height.
Slightly bend your elbows and retract/depress your shoulder blades to stabilize the shoulders before starting the movement.
Exhale and drive the handles upward and inward in a controlled, wide arc, leading with the elbows while maintaining the slight elbow bend.
Bring the handles together above the upper chest and hold a brief squeeze (about 1 second) focusing on contracting the pectorals.

Inhale and slowly reverse the motion, resisting the weight as you lower the handles back to the starting position until you feel a controlled stretch in the chest—do not let the shoulders shrug or the elbows fully lock.
Repeat for the prescribed number of repetitions, keeping tempo controlled and avoiding momentum or excessive range that causes shoulder discomfort.
exercises.description: Seated incline lever fly performed on an incline lever machine to isolate the upper portion of the pectorals by moving the arms in a wide arc while the torso is supported on an incline pad.

exercises.name: Cable Low Fly
exercises.notes: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single-hand handles.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single-hand handles.
Stand centered between the towers in an upright position with feet shoulder-width apart or one foot slightly staggered for stability.
Grasp the handles with a neutral grip so the cables run low behind your legs; step forward slightly to create tension in the cables and keep a tall chest.
Keep a slight bend in the elbows throughout the exercise (soft elbows) and a small forward lean from the hips; do not round the shoulders.
Exhale and pull both handles upward and inward in a controlled wide arc until your hands meet or nearly meet at chest level, squeezing the pectorals at the end of the motion.
Hold the peak contraction briefly, keeping the shoulders down and scapulae stable (use serratus anterior to stabilize the shoulder blades).
Inhale and slowly reverse the movement, allowing the cables to return your arms to the start position with control — do not let the weights slam.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining posture, a consistent elbow angle, and steady tempo throughout.
exercises.description: Standing cable low fly performed with the pulleys set low; you pull each handle upward and inward in a wide arc to bring the hands together in front of the chest, emphasizing horizontal adduction to work the pectorals. Maintain a slight elbow bend and controlled tempo throughout the movement.

exercises.name: Lever Pec Deck Fly
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat height so the handles/pads are level with the mid-chest; sit upright with your back flat against the pad and feet flat on the floor.
instructions: Adjust the seat height so the handles/pads are level with the mid-chest; sit upright with your back flat against the pad and feet flat on the floor.
Grasp the handles or place your forearms against the pads with a slight bend in the elbows; start with arms out to the sides and shoulder blades pulled back and down.
Exhale and drive the handles forward and together in a smooth arc by contracting the pectorals, keeping the elbows slightly bent and wrists neutral.
Pause briefly when the hands/handles meet or are directly in front of your chest, feeling the squeeze in the pectorals.
Inhale and slowly allow the levers to return to the start position under control, maintaining shoulder blade stability and avoiding rapid letting go or trunk leaning.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions; use a weight that allows full control of both concentric and eccentric phases without excessive shoulder or back movement.
exercises.description: Seated machine chest fly performed on a lever/pec-deck: you bring the arms together in front of the chest against resistance to isolate the pectoral muscles. The movement uses a slight elbow bend and controlled arc to emphasize the inner chest.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
Grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width, wrists stacked over elbows and thumbs wrapped around the bar.
Retract and depress the shoulder blades (pinch the shoulder blades together) to create a stable chest platform.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar over the chest so it is balanced and steady.
Inhale, lower the bar under control to the mid-chest (sternum) keeping elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso.
Pause briefly with the bar touching or just above the chest while maintaining tightness through the core and legs.
Exhale and drive the bar upward by extending the elbows, pressing the bar back to the start position while keeping wrists neutral.

exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a supine pressing exercise performed on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also working the triceps and anterior delts. The lifter lowers a barbell to the chest and presses it back to full arm extension under control.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45° and sit with a dumbbell in each hand resting on your thighs.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45° and sit with a dumbbell in each hand resting on your thighs.
Lie back so your upper back and head are supported (supine on the incline bench) and plant your feet flat on the floor for stability.
Bring the dumbbells up to shoulder level with palms facing forward and elbows bent (upper arms roughly 45° from the torso) while retracting the shoulder blades slightly.
Unrack the dumbbells by pressing them up and then lowering them to start position so the handles are beside your upper chest/shoulder area.
Press the dumbbells upward and slightly together until your arms are nearly straight (avoid locking or hyperextending the elbows); exhale during the press.
Pause briefly at the top with tension in the pectorals, then slowly lower the dumbbells under control back to the upper chest/shoulder level while inhaling.
Keep a stable core and avoid excessive arching of the lower back; maintain scapular control and smooth, controlled reps for the desired number of repetitions.
If using heavy weights, have a spotter or perform a controlled seated dumbbell rack/unrack to avoid strain when starting or finishing the set.
exercises.description: A pressing movement performed lying supine on an incline bench that emphasizes the upper portion of the chest. Dumbbells allow a slightly greater range of motion and independent arm movement compared with a barbell.

exercises.name: Lever Seated Fly
exercises.notes: Set the seat height so the handles are at mid-chest level when seated; select an appropriate weight and sit with your back firmly against the pad and feet flat on the floor.
instructions: Set the seat height so the handles are at mid-chest level when seated; select an appropriate weight and sit with your back firmly against the pad and feet flat on the floor.
Grasp the handles with a neutral grip, open your chest, keep a slight bend in the elbows, and position your upper arms roughly parallel to the floor (seated, upright posture).
Exhale and squeeze your pectorals to bring the handles together in a controlled arc until your hands/handles meet or nearly meet in front of your chest; avoid using momentum.
Pause briefly at the peak contraction, focusing on a chest squeeze rather than pressing with the arms or shoulders.
Inhale and slowly allow the handles to return to the starting wide position under control, maintaining the slight elbow bend and keeping your back against the pad.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions; avoid locking the elbows, leaning forward, or shrugging the shoulders during the movement.
exercises.description: Seated lever fly is a machine chest fly that isolates the pectoral muscles by bringing the arms together in a horizontal arc from an open-arm start position while seated with the back supported. It emphasizes a controlled concentric squeeze of the chest and a slow eccentric return.

exercises.name: Cable Lying Fly
exercises.notes: Set two cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single-handle grips; select a light–moderate weight you can control through the full range.
instructions: Set two cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single-handle grips; select a light–moderate weight you can control through the full range.
Lie supine (face up) on a flat bench placed between the two pulley towers with feet flat on the floor for stability.
Reach down and grab one handle in each hand with a neutral or palms-facing position; start with arms out to the sides at about shoulder height and a slight bend in the elbows.
Retract and depress your shoulder blades (squeeze the shoulder blades together and down) to stabilize the shoulders before beginning each rep.

Exhale and bring both hands up and together in a wide arc over your chest, maintaining the slight elbow bend and focusing on squeezing the pectorals at the top; hands should meet or nearly meet above the sternum.

Inhale and slowly lower the handles back out to the starting position in a controlled manner until you feel a gentle stretch across the chest — do not let the elbows drop excessively behind the line of the shoulders.
Keep the torso stable and avoid arching the lower back; maintain control of the cables throughout the entire set and adjust weight if you cannot control the return.
Perform the desired number of repetitions, then carefully release the handles and sit up before changing weight or leaving the bench.
exercises.description: A supine cable fly performed on a flat bench using low-set pulleys to isolate the chest by bringing the hands together in a wide arc. The movement emphasizes horizontal shoulder adduction while keeping a slight bend in the elbows to protect the joints.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Fly
exercises.notes: Set an adjustable bench to a 30–45° incline and sit with a dumbbell in each hand resting on your thighs.
instructions: Set an adjustable bench to a 30–45° incline and sit with a dumbbell in each hand resting on your thighs.
Lie back so your head, upper back and glutes are supported (supine on an incline bench) and place your feet flat on the floor for stability.
Use your legs to help rack the dumbbells and press them overhead so your arms are extended above your chest, palms facing each other (neutral grip); keep a slight bend in the elbows.
Retract your shoulder blades slightly and maintain a neutral spine throughout the movement; avoid excessive lower-back arching.

Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells in a wide, controlled arc out to your sides until you feel a stretch in the upper chest—stop when your elbows are roughly in line with the bench or slightly below shoulder level.
Keep the elbows fixed at the same slight bend throughout the descent; do not let the elbows drop or the wrists bend excessively.
Exhale and bring the dumbbells back together over your chest by squeezing the pectorals, following the same arc path used on the way down.
Pause briefly at the top with the weights close together (but avoid slamming them), then repeat for the desired number of reps.
Use a lighter weight if you feel shoulder joint pain; focus on controlled range of motion and chest contraction rather than heavy load.
exercises.description: Lying on an incline bench, perform a wide-arc dumbbell fly to emphasize the upper portion of the chest; movement stretches the chest as you lower and contracts the pectorals as you bring the weights back together. Keep a slight bend in the elbows and move in a controlled arc to avoid shoulder strain.

exercises.name: Cable Upper Chest Crossovers
exercises.notes: Set both cable pulleys to a high position (above shoulder height) and attach single-handle grips.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to a high position (above shoulder height) and attach single-handle grips.
Stand centered between the stations with a staggered or shoulder-width stance for stability and grab a handle in each hand (palms facing down or slightly rotated), arms out to the sides.
Hinge slightly forward at the hips so your torso is upright but leaning a bit toward the floor; keep chest up, shoulders down and back, and a slight bend in the elbows throughout.

Exhale and pull both handles in a controlled downward and inward arc, bringing your hands together in front of the upper chest/clavicle area (hands may cross slightly) while actively squeezing the pectorals.
Pause briefly at the peak contraction, keeping the torso stable and avoiding shoulder rotation or shrugging.
Inhale and slowly return the handles along the same arc back to the start position with control, resisting the weight rather than letting it pull you back.
Keep core engaged and avoid using momentum; choose a weight that allows full range of motion with good form.
exercises.description: Standing cable crossovers performed with the pulleys set high to emphasize the upper chest; the movement brings the handles down and together in a downward arc to target the clavicular portion of the pectorals. Maintain a slight forward lean and a small bend in the elbows while squeezing the chest at the finish.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Low Fly
exercises.notes: Start standing with feet hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees soft and a slight hinge at the hips (standing, slight forward lean).
instructions: Start standing with feet hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees soft and a slight hinge at the hips (standing, slight forward lean).
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral grip at or just above thigh level (low position). Keep the spine neutral and shoulders down.
Maintain a small, fixed bend in the elbows throughout the movement; do not lock the joints.
Exhale and raise the dumbbells in a wide arc up and inward, leading with the elbows so the hands meet or come close in front of the upper chest/shoulder level (low-to-high arc).
Pause briefly at the top and actively squeeze the pectorals while keeping the shoulders stable (avoid shrugging).
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back along the same arc to the starting low position under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, keeping tempo controlled and avoiding momentum or excessive back extension.
exercises.description: A standing low-to-high dumbbell fly that moves the weights in an upward arc from near the hips to in front of the chest, emphasizing the pectorals. Keep a slight bend in the elbows and squeeze the chest at the top of the movement.

exercises.name: Lever Pec Deck Fly
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat height so the handles/pads are level with the mid-chest; sit upright with your back flat against the pad and feet flat on the floor.
instructions: Adjust the seat height so the handles/pads are level with the mid-chest; sit upright with your back flat against the pad and feet flat on the floor.
Grasp the handles or place your forearms against the pads with a slight bend in the elbows; start with arms out to the sides and shoulder blades pulled back and down.

exercises.name: Barbell Pendlay Row
exercises.notes: Grip the bar with a double overhand or mixed grip about shoulder-width apart, keep a neutral spine and eyes looking a few feet ahead on the floor.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart and the barbell over mid-foot; bend at the hips and knees to reach the bar so your torso is roughly parallel to the floor (hip hinge), knees slightly bent.
Grip the bar with a double overhand or mixed grip about shoulder-width apart, keep a neutral spine and eyes looking a few feet ahead on the floor.
Brace your core, pull your shoulder blades down and back, then explosively row the bar in a straight line to your lower chest/upper abdomen, driving your elbows back.
Squeeze your upper back at the top for 1 second while keeping the torso stable (no rowing with the hips or excessive torso rotation).
Lower the bar under control all the way back to the floor so it comes to a complete stop, reset any slack, then repeat for the desired reps.
Maintain a flat back and braced core throughout; if your lower back rounds or you must use momentum, reduce weight or raise the bar height.
exercises.description: The Barbell Pendlay Row is a strict horizontal barbell row performed from a dead stop on the floor each rep, emphasizing the upper back and lats while minimizing torso momentum. It uses a hip hinge with a flat back and pulls the bar to the lower chest/upper abdomen before returning it to the floor.

exercises.name: Lever T-bar Row (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Stand on the machine foot platforms with feet hip-width apart and straddle the bar, grasping the handles with a neutral or overhand grip.
instructions: Stand on the machine foot platforms with feet hip-width apart and straddle the bar, grasping the handles with a neutral or overhand grip.
Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is inclined forward (maintain a neutral spine and avoid rounding), and brace your core.
Begin with your arms extended and shoulders protracted so the weight is under control.
Exhale and pull the handles toward your lower chest/upper abdomen by driving your elbows back and squeezing your shoulder blades together.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining scapular retraction and keeping the torso stable (avoid excessive torso lean or momentum).
Inhale and slowly lower the handles back to the start under control until arms are nearly extended, keeping tension in the back throughout.
Repeat for the desired reps, keeping movements smooth and avoiding shrugging the shoulders or rounding the lower back.
Adjust foot position or torso angle as needed to maintain a comfortable, neutral spine and full range of motion.
exercises.description: A bent-over plate-loaded T-bar row where you stand on the foot platform and hinge at the hips to pull the handle toward your torso, primarily working the lats and mid-back. The movement emphasizes scapular retraction and elbow drive while maintaining a neutral spine.

exercises.name: Lever Pronated Grip Seated Scapula Retraction Shrug (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat height and load the machine so you can reach the handles with your arms extended and feet flat on the footrests; sit upright with a neutral spine and knees bent.
instructions: Adjust the seat height and load the machine so you can reach the handles with your arms extended and feet flat on the footrests; sit upright with a neutral spine and knees bent.
Grasp the handles with a pronated (overhand) grip and let your shoulders relax down and slightly forward to set the start position.
Initiate the movement by pulling the handles back using your shoulder blades: actively retract (pinch) the scapulae together while keeping the torso stable and chest neutral.
As you retract the scapulae, elevate your shoulders into a controlled shrug (shoulder elevation) and squeeze your traps and upper back at the top for 1–2 seconds.
Slowly reverse the motion: lower the shoulders and allow the scapulae to protract back to the start position under control — do not let the weight slam forward.
Keep the arms relatively straight with a slight bend at the elbows, avoid using momentum or torso swing, and breathe out during the contraction and in during the return.
exercises.description: Seated, pronated-grip plate-loaded exercise that emphasizes scapular retraction and an upward shrug to target the trapezius and upper back. Performed from a seated position using the machine handles to actively pinch the shoulder blades together and elevate the shoulders.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly 45 degrees to parallel to the floor; keep the spine neutral (no rounding) and engage the abs and glutes.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and the barbell over mid-foot; use an overhand (pronated) grip slightly wider than shoulder width.
Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly 45 degrees to parallel to the floor; keep the spine neutral (no rounding) and engage the abs and glutes.
Hold the bar with arms extended and shoulders protracted, chest up and eyes looking a few feet in front of you to maintain a neutral neck.
Initiate the rep by driving the elbows back and up, pulling the bar toward your lower chest / upper abdomen while squeezing the shoulder blades together.
Keep the elbows close to the body (not flaring excessively) and avoid using momentum from the hips; breathe out as you pull.
Lower the bar slowly and under control to the starting position with arms extended while maintaining the hip hinge and neutral spine; breathe in on the descent.
Repeat for the desired reps, controlling tempo and keeping the torso stable — if you cannot maintain a flat back, reduce weight or stop and reset.
exercises.description: Standing hip-hinged barbell row where you pull a loaded barbell toward the lower chest/upper abdomen; primarily targets the lats and upper back while requiring posterior chain and core stabilization. Maintain a flat back and drive the elbows back to retract the shoulder blades.

exercises.name: Lever Seated Row
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat and chest pad (if present) so your feet are flat on the platform and the handles are within comfortable reach with your arms extended.
instructions: Adjust the seat and chest pad (if present) so your feet are flat on the platform and the handles are within comfortable reach with your arms extended.
Select an appropriate weight on the lever stack.
Sit upright with your chest against the pad (or seated tall if no pad), knees slightly bent, feet stable, and maintain a neutral spine and head alignment.
Reach forward and grasp the handles with a firm, neutral grip; start with your arms fully extended and shoulders relaxed.
Initiate the pull by driving your elbows backward and slightly down, retracting your shoulder blades to bring the handles toward your lower chest/upper abdomen.
Keep elbows close to your sides, avoid excessive torso lean or shrugging the shoulders, and breathe out as you pull.
Pause briefly at the peak contraction and actively squeeze your lats and upper back.
Slowly extend your arms to return the handles to the start position under control, allowing the shoulder blades to protract slightly but maintain tension.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining neutral spine and controlled tempo throughout.
exercises.description: Seated lever rows are a horizontal pulling exercise performed on a lever machine that primarily targets the lats while also engaging the middle and upper back and arm muscles. The movement involves pulling handles toward the torso with a scapular retraction and controlled return.

exercises.name: Cable Low Seated Row with V bar
exercises.notes: Sit tall with a slight forward lean from the hips, chest lifted, core engaged and shoulders relaxed; allow the arms to fully extend so the weight is supported by the arms and scapulae are slightly protracted.
instructions: Sit on the bench facing the low cable pulley with feet braced on the footplate and knees slightly bent; grasp the V‑bar with a neutral (palms facing) grip.

Sit tall with a slight forward lean from the hips, chest lifted, core engaged and shoulders relaxed; allow the arms to fully extend so the weight is supported by the arms and scapulae are slightly protracted.
Initiate the movement by pulling the elbows straight back toward your torso (not flaring wide), driving the elbows past your sides and pulling the V‑bar to your lower chest/upper abdomen.
At the end range, squeeze your shoulder blades together and hold the contraction for 1–2 seconds while keeping the torso stable and upright.
Slowly extend the arms forward in a controlled manner, allowing the scapulae to re‑protract and return to the start position without rounding the lower back.
Breathe out while pulling, inhale while returning; avoid using momentum or leaning excessively backward—keep the motion controlled and driven by the back and arms.
exercises.description: Seated low cable row using a V‑bar is a horizontal pulling exercise that emphasizes the middle/upper back by driving the elbows back and squeezing the shoulder blades, while also engaging the lats and arm muscles. The neutral V‑grip shortens the lever and increases biceps involvement.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Single Arm Shrug
exercises.notes: Stand upright (feet about shoulder-width), hold a dumbbell in one hand with the arm fully extended at your side and palm facing the body; keep a neutral head and spine.
instructions: Stand upright (feet about shoulder-width), hold a dumbbell in one hand with the arm fully extended at your side and palm facing the body; keep a neutral head and spine.
Brace your core, keep the elbow straight (but not locked) and shoulders relaxed before initiating the movement.
Exhale and elevate (shrug) the shoulder of the working arm straight up toward the ear, using only the shoulder girdle—do not roll or rotate the shoulder.
Pause and squeeze the traps at the top for 1–2 seconds, focusing on the contraction near the neck.
Inhale and slowly lower the shoulder back to the starting position under control until the arm is fully extended and the trap is relaxed.
Perform the desired number of repetitions on one side, then switch arms; maintain neutral head position and avoid using momentum throughout the set.
exercises.description: A standing single-arm dumbbell shrug that elevates the shoulder to target the upper trapezius and surrounding neck muscles. Performed with one arm at a time to concentrate on trap contraction and improve unilateral strength and symmetry.

exercises.name: Cable one arm lat pulldown
exercises.notes: Setup: Attach a single D-handle to the high pulley and sit facing the machine on the seat with knees secured under the pads; sit upright with feet flat on the floor.
instructions: Setup: Attach a single D-handle to the high pulley and sit facing the machine on the seat with knees secured under the pads; sit upright with feet flat on the floor.
Grip and position: Reach up and grasp the handle with a neutral or overhand grip using one hand; keep the torso tall with a slight backward lean from the hips and brace your core.

Start position: Allow the arm to be fully extended overhead while maintaining scapular stability (shoulder blades slightly down and back), and keep the opposite hand resting or lightly stabilizing the thigh.
Pull: Exhale and pull the handle down and back by driving your elbow toward your hip/side of the torso, focusing on squeezing the lats and retracting the shoulder blade.
Peak contraction: Pause briefly at the bottom when your hand reaches about chest/hip level and you feel a strong contraction in the lats; avoid rotating the torso or shrugging the shoulder.
Return: Inhale and slowly control the handle back up to the fully extended start position, resisting the weight to maintain tension throughout the eccentric phase.
Repetitions and switch: Perform the desired number of repetitions on one side, then switch to the other arm to maintain balance.
exercises.description: Seated single-arm cable pulldown uses a high pulley and single handle to pull one arm down and back, isolating the lats while encouraging unilateral strength and balance. The movement emphasizes pulling the elbow toward the hip/side of the torso with a controlled tempo.

exercises.name: Cable Pulldown
exercises.notes: Set the pulley to the highest position and attach the pro-lat bar; select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Set the pulley to the highest position and attach the pro-lat bar; select an appropriate weight.
Sit on the bench with knees secured under the pads and feet flat on the floor (seated position).
Reach up and grasp the bar with a wide overhand grip, hands slightly wider than shoulder-width.
Sit tall with a neutral spine and chest slightly lifted; lean back only a few degrees (small recline) — do not arch the lower back.
Initiate the movement by driving your elbows down and back, pulling the bar toward the upper chest/clavicle while leading with the elbows and squeezing the shoulder blades together.
Hold and squeeze the lats briefly at the bottom of the movement without leaning excessively backward.
Slowly and under control allow the bar to return to the start position until the arms are nearly fully extended and the shoulder blades re-expand.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, maintaining controlled tempo and avoiding momentum or swinging.
exercises.description: Seated cable pulldown using a pro-lat bar targets the latissimus dorsi with a wide overhand grip, pulling the bar down toward the upper chest. The movement also engages the biceps, rear delts and upper back muscles to stabilize and complete the pull.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.notes: Light weight, focus on form and range of motion

warmup.notes: Brief warmup to activate target muscles. Can skip if first working set feels warm.

exercises.name: Barbell Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips, push the butt back, and bend the knees slightly so the torso is angled forward (near parallel to the floor), keeping a neutral spine and head in line with the spine.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart and the barbell over the midfoot; grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width using an overhand or mixed grip.
Hinge at the hips, push the butt back, and bend the knees slightly so the torso is angled forward (near parallel to the floor), keeping a neutral spine and head in line with the spine.
Brace your core and retract your shoulder blades slightly to set the upper back before the first rep.
Pull the barbell upward toward the lower chest/upper abdomen by driving the elbows back and keeping them close to the body; focus on squeezing the upper back at the top of the movement.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining a flat back, then lower the barbell under control until arms are nearly extended while preserving the hip-hinge position.
Breathe out while pulling the bar up and inhale while lowering it; keep the torso stable and avoid rounding the lower back or using excessive torso momentum.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, and reset your hip-hinge position between sets if you lose posture.
exercises.description: A hip-hinged barbell row that trains the upper back by pulling the loaded barbell toward the torso while maintaining a neutral spine. It also recruits the lats, arms and posterior chain for stability and power.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bent over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips until your torso is leaning forward (torso roughly parallel to the floor or about 30–45° above parallel), knees slightly bent; this is a bent-over position — brace your core and keep a neutral spine.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart and hold a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral (palms facing) grip.

Hinge at the hips until your torso is leaning forward (torso roughly parallel to the floor or about 30–45° above parallel), knees slightly bent; this is a bent-over position — brace your core and keep a neutral spine.
Let the dumbbells hang straight down from your shoulders with your arms fully extended; keep your head aligned with your spine.
Initiate the pull by driving your elbows back and up, pulling the dumbbells toward your lower ribs or hip area while squeezing your shoulder blades together.
Pause briefly at the top of the movement, maintaining tightness through the lats and upper back without shrugging the shoulders excessively.
Slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position with controlled motion until your arms are extended.
Repeat for the prescribed reps, keeping the torso fixed (avoid using momentum or excessive torso rotation) and continue to breathe: exhale while pulling, inhale while lowering.
exercises.description: A bent-over row performed with dumbbells where you hinge at the hips and pull the weights toward your torso, emphasizing the lats and upper back. The movement also engages the arms and posterior chain to stabilize the hinged position.

exercises.name: Cable Low Seated Row
exercises.notes: Sit upright on the bench or seat with your feet braced against the footplate and knees slightly bent; reach forward and grasp the low cable handle with a neutral or overhand grip.
instructions: Sit upright on the bench or seat with your feet braced against the footplate and knees slightly bent; reach forward and grasp the low cable handle with a neutral or overhand grip.
Start with your torso leaning slightly forward at the hips, chest up, shoulders relaxed and spine neutral — this is the starting position.
Exhale and pull the handle toward your lower chest/upper abdomen by driving your elbows straight back, leading the movement with your elbows rather than your hands.
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the end of the pull and hold the peak contraction for a brief count, keeping your torso stable (avoid excessive leaning or momentum).
Inhale and slowly extend your arms to return the handle to the starting position with control, allowing the lats and upper back to lengthen fully.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, maintaining a neutral spine, steady tempo, and controlled range of motion throughout.
exercises.description: Seated cable low row performed from a bench with feet braced; you pull the handle toward your torso to drive scapular retraction and elbow extension, focusing on the muscles of the back. Controlled return lengthens the lats and upper back while the arms and forearms assist.

exercises.name: Cable Rear Pulldown
exercises.notes: Adjust the thigh pads so your legs are secured and select an appropriate weight on the stack.
instructions: Adjust the thigh pads so your legs are secured and select an appropriate weight on the stack.
Sit upright on the bench with feet flat, chest up and head in a neutral position; reach overhead and grasp a wide curved lat bar with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder width.
Lean back very slightly at the hips (small forward lean is acceptable for comfort), keep the chest lifted and scapulae retracted before initiating the pull.
Exhale and pull the bar down behind your head toward the upper trapezius/occipital area by driving the elbows down and back, focusing on squeezing the lats and shoulder blades together.
Pause briefly at the bottom with control, avoiding excessive neck extension or forward head movement.
Inhale and slowly allow the bar to return to the fully extended start position with controlled tension in the lats; do not let the weight stack slam.
Repeat for the desired reps. Use a front pulldown variation instead if you have shoulder pain or limited mobility to reduce risk.
exercises.description: Seated cable pulldown where the bar is pulled down behind the head to target the large back muscles. Emphasizes the lats and upper posterior shoulder/upper back while the arms act as secondary drivers.

exercises.name: Cable Reverse Grip Pulldown
exercises.notes: Select an appropriate weight, then reach up and grasp the bar with a shoulder-width underhand (supinated) grip, palms facing you.
instructions: Sit on the lat pulldown bench with your feet flat on the floor and knees secured under the thigh pads.
Select an appropriate weight, then reach up and grasp the bar with a shoulder-width underhand (supinated) grip, palms facing you.
Sit upright with a slight lean back (about 10–20 degrees), chest up, and scapulae slightly retracted; maintain a neutral spine throughout.
Inhale, brace your core, then exhale and pull the bar down by driving your elbows down and back until the bar reaches your upper chest or collarbone level.
Squeeze your lats and upper back at the bottom of the motion while keeping wrists neutral and avoiding excessive neck or shoulder shrugging.
Slowly control the weight back to the start by allowing the arms to extend fully overhead while maintaining scapular tension (do not fully relax shoulders between reps).
Repeat for the desired reps. Breathe out on the pull and inhale on the return, and avoid using momentum or excessive torso lean.
exercises.description: A seated cable pulldown performed with a supinated (reverse/underhand) grip that emphasizes the lats while increasing biceps involvement. Pull the bar from an overhead position down to the upper chest while keeping the torso stable and shoulders retracted.

exercises.name: Cable Straight Arm Pulldown
exercises.notes: Set a high pulley with a straight bar or rope attachment and select a light-to-moderate weight that allows strict form.
instructions: Set a high pulley with a straight bar or rope attachment and select a light-to-moderate weight that allows strict form.

Stand facing the machine about 1–2 feet away, feet shoulder-width apart and knees slightly bent; hinge forward at the hips so your torso is angled forward (standing bent-over position) with a neutral spine.
Reach up and grasp the bar with an overhand grip (palms facing down) about shoulder-width apart; maintain a small, natural bend in the elbows throughout (do not lock).
Brace your core and depress your shoulder blades slightly (avoid shrugging) so tension is felt in the lats before starting the pull.

Exhale and pull the bar down in a controlled arc by extending the shoulders and pulling with the lats until the bar reaches your upper thighs or mid-thigh level; keep the arms long and the elbows fixed in their slight bend.
Squeeze the lats and hold the bottom position briefly while keeping the ribs down and spine neutral.
Inhale and slowly allow the bar to rise back to the start position under control, resisting the cable so the lats remain under tension; do not let the lower back round as you return.
Repeat for the desired reps, maintaining the hip hinge, neutral spine, and long-arm position on every repetition.
exercises.description: Standing cable straight-arm pulldowns hinge from the hips and pull a high cable down with mostly straight arms to load the lats through a long-range extension. The movement emphasizes scapular depression and shoulder extension while keeping the torso braced.

exercises.name: Cable Cross over Lateral Pulldown
exercises.notes: Setup: Attach single D-handles to both high pulleys of a cable crossover or set a straight bar on the high pulley; select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Setup: Attach single D-handles to both high pulleys of a cable crossover or set a straight bar on the high pulley; select an appropriate weight.

Starting position: Stand between the towers or slightly in front of them with feet shoulder-width apart, knees soft, and a slight forward lean from the hips; grasp the handles with an overhand or neutral grip at shoulder height.
Brace your core and pull the shoulder blades down and back to initiate the movement.
Execution: Pull the handles down and slightly across your body by driving your elbows toward your sides and back, leading with the elbows rather than the hands.
Continue the pull until your hands are roughly at or just below chest level and you feel a full contraction in the lats and upper back; squeeze your shoulder blades together at the bottom.
Return: Slowly allow the cables to raise your hands back to the start position with controlled tension, keeping the torso stable and core engaged.
Coaching tips: Avoid excessive torso rotation or using momentum; keep a slight forward lean and focus on elbow-driven movement. Breathe out on the pull and inhale on the return.
exercises.description: Standing cable pulldown from the high crossover position, pulling the handles down and slightly across the body to load the latissimus dorsi and upper-back musculature. Performed from a slight forward-leaning stance while driving the elbows down and back and squeezing the shoulder blades.

exercises.name: Cable Shrug
exercises.notes: Stand facing the cable machine with a handle attached to a low or mid pulley; feet shoulder-width apart and weight evenly distributed.
instructions: Stand facing the cable machine with a handle attached to a low or mid pulley; feet shoulder-width apart and weight evenly distributed.
Grasp one or two handles with a neutral (palms facing the body) or overhand grip and let your arms hang fully extended at your sides; keep a neutral neck and spine.
Slightly bend the knees and brace your core to stabilize the torso; do not lean forward or backward.
Exhale and elevate (shrug) your shoulders straight up toward your ears, focusing on contracting the trapezius—do not roll or rotate the shoulders.
Hold the peak contraction briefly (about 1 second) while maintaining straight arms and a neutral neck.
Inhale and slowly lower the shoulders back down to the start in a controlled manner until traps are fully stretched.
Repeat for the desired reps, keeping movement strict and avoiding momentum; reduce weight if you must use arm or torso movement to lift.
exercises.description: Standing cable shrugs target the trapezius by elevating the shoulders against resistance from a cable. Performed with arms straight at the sides, the movement emphasizes a vertical shrug with controlled lowering.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Overhead Press
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet about shoulder-width apart, knees slightly soft (not locked).
instructions: Stand tall with feet about shoulder-width apart, knees slightly soft (not locked).
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height with palms facing forward or slightly neutral and elbows roughly under the wrists.
Brace your core and squeeze your glutes to maintain a neutral spine and prevent lower-back arching.
Exhale and press both dumbbells straight overhead in a controlled path until your arms are nearly fully extended (avoid jamming the elbows).
Keep the wrists stacked over the elbows and the chin tucked slightly; do not overextend the neck or lower back at the top.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining tension in the delts and scapular stabilizers.
Inhale and lower the dumbbells slowly and under control back to the starting shoulder-height position.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, maintaining core tension and full control on both ascent and descent.
exercises.description: A standing dumbbell overhead press that primarily works the delts by pressing weights from shoulder height to full arm extension overhead, while the triceps and upper-back muscles stabilize and assist.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Arnold Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor, knees bent at ~90°, and core braced; hold a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder level with palms facing your torso and elbows bent.
instructions: Sit upright on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor, knees bent at ~90°, and core braced; hold a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder level with palms facing your torso and elbows bent.
Keep your chest up and back neutral (avoid excessive lower-back arch) and prepare to press by rotating your wrists outward so your palms will face forward at the top of the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells overhead while simultaneously rotating your palms until the arms are extended overhead and palms face forward; do not lock or hyperextend the elbows.
Pause briefly at the top with the weights controlled overhead, maintaining tension in the delts and triceps.
Inhale and lower the dumbbells back down in a controlled manner while rotating the wrists back to the starting position with palms facing your torso and elbows returning to shoulder level.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, keeping movement smooth and avoiding using leg drive or excessive torso lean to lift the weights.
exercises.description: A seated rotational dumbbell press that works the shoulder muscles through a palms-in to palms-forward rotation, emphasizing the delts while also engaging the triceps and upper back. Performed seated to limit torso drive and increase shoulder isolation.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent and core braced (standing position).
instructions: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent and core braced (standing position).
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with a neutral grip (palms facing your body) and wrists straight.
Keep a small bend in the elbows throughout the movement and maintain a neutral spine and chest up.
Exhale and raise both arms out to the sides by lifting at the shoulders, leading with the elbows so the hands finish roughly at shoulder height (arms nearly parallel to the floor).
Avoid shrugging the shoulders up toward the ears; keep the neck long and scapulae stable to minimize excessive trap involvement.
Pause briefly (0–1 second) at the top while maintaining tension in the delts.
Inhale and lower the dumbbells slowly and controlled back to the starting position at your sides.
Repeat for desired reps, keeping movement smooth and avoiding momentum or swinging; use lighter weight if form breaks down.
exercises.description: Standing dumbbell lateral raise works the lateral delts by lifting the arms out to the sides to about shoulder height, with a slight elbow bend and controlled motion. It improves shoulder width and isolates the middle shoulder fibers while the traps and forearms assist.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.notes: Light weight, focus on form and range of motion

warmup.notes: Brief warmup to activate target muscles. Can skip if first working set feels warm.

exercises.name: Barbell Standing Military Press
exercises.notes: Stand upright with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and the barbell resting on your front shoulders/clavicle in a front-rack position (hands just outside shoulder width, elbows slightly forward).
instructions: Stand upright with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and the barbell resting on your front shoulders/clavicle in a front-rack position (hands just outside shoulder width, elbows slightly forward).
Grip the bar with a firm overhand grip, wrists stacked under the bar and chest tall; take a full breath and brace your core and glutes to prevent lower-back arching.
Tighten your upper back and scapulae (retract slightly) and press your head back just enough to allow the bar to travel in a straight line.

Drive the barbell upward forcefully by extending the elbows and pressing through the shoulders until the arms are fully extended overhead; finish with the bar roughly over the midline of the body and the head moved slightly forward under the bar.
Pause briefly at the top with shoulders engaged and core braced, then inhale and lower the bar under control back to the starting front-rack position at the clavicle.
Perform repetitions with a steady tempo, keeping the torso rigid and avoiding excessive lumbar extension or forward collapse of the elbows.

If starting from the floor (no rack), use a controlled clean or push-press to bring the bar to the front-rack before beginning strict presses; lower weight to the floor only after finishing the set or rerack safely.
exercises.description: A standing barbell overhead press performed from the front-rack position that primarily develops the shoulder (delts) musculature while requiring upper-body pressing strength and core stability. Performed without a rack, the bar is pressed vertically from the shoulders to full arm extension overhead.

exercises.name: Barbell Clean and Press
exercises.notes: Step 1: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart, toes slightly turned out, barbell over mid-foot; grip the bar just outside the knees with a full overhand or mixed grip and keep the chest up and core braced (standing position).
instructions: Step 1: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart, toes slightly turned out, barbell over mid-foot; grip the bar just outside the knees with a full overhand or mixed grip and keep the chest up and core braced (standing position).
Step 2: Initiate the lift with a hip hinge by bending at the hips and knees keeping the spine neutral and shoulders slightly in front of the bar; maintain a tight core and engaged lats.

Step 3: Drive through the heels and extend the hips, knees and ankles explosively (triple extension) to accelerate the bar upward while shrugging the shoulders and pulling the elbows high and out (clean pull).

Step 4: As the bar reaches peak upward momentum, quickly drop under the bar into a partial front squat and rotate the elbows forward to receive the bar on the front of the shoulders in a front-rack position (clean, front-rack).

Step 5: Stand tall from the front-rack by extending the hips and knees to full standing, reset your core and grip, then press the bar overhead by extending the elbows and driving the head slightly forward until arms are fully locked out (press).
Step 6: Lower the bar under control back to the front-rack position, then carefully return the bar to the floor with a hip hinge and bend the knees to reset for the next repetition.

Step 7: Maintain braced abs and a neutral spine throughout, breathe consistently (exhale during the press), and use controlled tempo—perform the clean explosively but receive and press with stability and control.
exercises.description: A compound Olympic-style lift combining a barbell clean (lifting the bar to a front-rack position) followed immediately by a press overhead, training the shoulders along with legs, hips and upper back. It develops explosive power, pressing strength, and full-body coordination.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent and core braced (standing position).
instructions: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent and core braced (standing position).
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with a neutral grip (palms facing your body) and wrists straight.
Keep a small bend in the elbows throughout the movement and maintain a neutral spine and chest up.
Exhale and raise both arms out to the sides by lifting at the shoulders, leading with the elbows so the hands finish roughly at shoulder height (arms nearly parallel to the floor).
Avoid shrugging the shoulders up toward the ears; keep the neck long and scapulae stable to minimize excessive trap involvement.
Pause briefly (0–1 second) at the top while maintaining tension in the delts.
Inhale and lower the dumbbells slowly and controlled back to the starting position at your sides.
Repeat for desired reps, keeping movement smooth and avoiding momentum or swinging; use lighter weight if form breaks down.
exercises.description: Standing dumbbell lateral raise works the lateral delts by lifting the arms out to the sides to about shoulder height, with a slight elbow bend and controlled motion. It improves shoulder width and isolates the middle shoulder fibers while the traps and forearms assist.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Rear Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench so the back pad is at a low angle and stand facing the side of the bench.
instructions: Set an incline bench so the back pad is at a low angle and stand facing the side of the bench.
Pick up a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral (palms facing) grip and take a hip-width stance with a slight bend in the knees.
Hinge forward at the hips and place your forehead or chest gently against the bench pad for support so your torso is close to horizontal (bench-assisted bent-over position).
Let the arms hang straight down from the shoulders with a small bend in the elbows; keep a neutral spine and the head supported on the bench.
Exhale and raise the dumbbells out to the sides in a wide arc — lead with the elbows — until your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor and you feel the rear delts contract.
Pause briefly at the top, then inhale and lower the dumbbells slowly and under control back to the starting position.
Keep the movement strict (no torso rotation or momentum) and maintain head/chest support on the bench throughout the set.
Choose a weight that allows controlled form for the full range of motion and perform recommended reps.
exercises.description: A bent-over, bench-supported dumbbell raise that targets the rear (posterior) delts by moving the arms out to the sides while the torso is stabilized. The head/chest is supported on an incline bench to reduce momentum and isolate the rear deltoids.

exercises.name: Cable Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single handles.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single handles. Stand centered between the two towers and grasp each handle with the opposite hand so the cables cross in front of you (or hold same-side handles if preferred).
Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart, knees soft, and core braced (neutral spine). Keep a slight bend in the elbows and wrists neutral.
Start with the handles low by your thighs; palms can face inward or slightly forward. Maintain a small forward lean from the hips if comfortable to allow a natural arc.
Keeping the elbows slightly bent, exhale and raise both arms out to the sides in a smooth arc until your hands reach approximately shoulder height. Lead the movement with the elbow, not the wrist.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining tension in the delts; avoid shrugging the shoulders upward toward your ears.
Inhale and lower the handles slowly and under control back to the starting position, keeping tension on the cables throughout the descent.
Repeat for the desired number of reps. Use a lighter weight if you cannot maintain strict form or if you begin using torso momentum.
exercises.description: A standing cable lateral raise performed from low pulleys to isolate the lateral head of the delts; cables provide constant tension while you lift the arms out to the sides to shoulder height. The movement is done with a slight elbow bend and controlled tempo to minimize momentum.

exercises.name: Cable Standing Face Pull
exercises.notes: Set a cable pulley to about eye- to upper-chest height and attach a rope handle.
instructions: Set a cable pulley to about eye- to upper-chest height and attach a rope handle.
Stand facing the machine with feet shoulder-width apart or in a slight staggered stance, knees soft and spine neutral.
Grasp the rope with both hands using a neutral/overhand grip (palms facing inward) and take a step back so there is tension on the cable.
Initiate the movement by retracting the shoulder blades and pulling the rope toward your face, driving the elbows out to the sides so your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor.
As you reach the end range, externally rotate the shoulders so the hands split apart and the rope ends are near your temples or ears; pause and squeeze the rear delts and upper back.
Slowly reverse the motion under control, extending the arms forward while maintaining scapular control until the hands return to the start position.
Repeat for the desired reps, keeping torso stable (no excessive leaning or momentum) and focusing on quality of movement and external rotation.
exercises.description: A standing cable face pull with a rope attachment that emphasizes the posterior delts and upper back by pulling the rope toward the face while externally rotating the shoulders. It develops rear shoulder strength, scapular retraction, and shoulder external rotators.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.

exercises.name: Barbell Standing Military Press
exercises.notes: Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart and knees slightly bent (soft), grip the barbell just wider than shoulder-width with palms facing forward.
instructions: Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart and knees slightly bent (soft), grip the barbell just wider than shoulder-width with palms facing forward.
Hold the bar at upper-chest / front-shoulder level (resting on front delts / clavicle area), elbows pointed slightly forward and directly under the wrists.
Brace your core and pull your shoulder blades down and back to stabilize the spine; keep a neutral head and avoid excessive arching of the lower back.
Inhale, then press the barbell straight up overhead by extending the elbows and driving through the delts; move the bar slightly back only enough to end with it stacked over mid-foot at full lockout.
Exhale at the top with arms fully extended but avoid hyperextending the elbows and lower back; keep the ribcage down and core tight.
Lower the bar under control back to the starting position at the upper chest, keeping wrists neutral and elbows tracking under the bar.
Repeat for desired reps. Use a spotter or lighter weight if not using a rack and maintain strict form to protect shoulders and spine.
exercises.description: A standing barbell press performed from the front shoulder/chest area to overhead, primarily working the shoulder delts while pressing the weight up in a vertical path. The lift requires core stabilization and arm extension to lock out overhead.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Overhead Press
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet hip-width apart, knees slightly bent, and core braced (standing position).
instructions: Stand tall with feet hip-width apart, knees slightly bent, and core braced (standing position).
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height with palms facing forward or neutral and elbows slightly in front of the body.
Keep the spine neutral and ribcage down to avoid hyperextending the lower back; squeeze the abs throughout.
Exhale and press both dumbbells upward in a controlled path until the arms are nearly fully extended overhead without locking the elbows.
Pause briefly at the top with shoulders stacked over hips and the head neutral (you may slightly move your head back to allow the dumbbells to pass), keeping shoulder blades stable.
Inhale and lower the dumbbells slowly back to the starting shoulder-height position under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, avoiding excessive torso lean or lower-back arching; if needed, use a lighter weight to maintain good form.
exercises.description: A standing dumbbell overhead press is a compound shoulder exercise where you press dumbbells from shoulder height to full arm extension overhead, primarily working the delts while engaging the triceps and upper back for support and stabilization.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Rear Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench so the back pad is at a low angle and stand facing the side of the bench.
instructions: Set an incline bench so the back pad is at a low angle and stand facing the side of the bench.
Pick up a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral (palms facing) grip and take a hip-width stance with a slight bend in the knees.
Hinge forward at the hips and place your forehead or chest gently against the bench pad for support so your torso is close to horizontal (bench-assisted bent-over position).
Let the arms hang straight down from the shoulders with a small bend in the elbows; keep a neutral spine and the head supported on the bench.
Exhale and raise the dumbbells out to the sides in a wide arc — lead with the elbows — until your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor and you feel the rear delts contract.
Pause briefly at the top, then inhale and lower the dumbbells slowly and under control back to the starting position.
Keep the movement strict (no torso rotation or momentum) and maintain head/chest support on the bench throughout the set.
Choose a weight that allows controlled form for the full range of motion and perform recommended reps.
exercises.description: A bent-over, bench-supported dumbbell raise that targets the rear (posterior) delts by moving the arms out to the sides while the torso is stabilized. The head/chest is supported on an incline bench to reduce momentum and isolate the rear deltoids.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Bent Arm Lateral raise
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet hip-width apart, knees soft and core braced (standing, upright position).
instructions: Stand tall with feet hip-width apart, knees soft and core braced (standing, upright position).
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral grip (palms facing in) and elbows bent about 90 degrees, dumbbells resting by your hips.
Keep a slight forward torso angle if comfortable, chest up and neck neutral; do not lean or arch the lower back.
Exhale and raise your forearms out to the sides by driving the elbows upward, leading with the elbows so the dumbbells travel in an arc to shoulder height (upper arms roughly parallel to the floor).
Pause briefly at the top while keeping the elbow bend constant and maintaining scapular control (avoid shrugging the shoulders).
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position with control, maintaining the bent elbow angle throughout.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, keeping movement strict—avoid using momentum or swinging the torso.
exercises.description: A standing lateral raise variation performed with elbows bent to reduce lever length and emphasize the lateral delts; dumbbells are lifted out to the sides with a neutral grip until the upper arms reach roughly parallel to the floor.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Lateral to Front Raise
exercises.notes: Stand upright (standing) with feet hip-width apart, knees soft, and core braced; hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with a neutral grip.
instructions: Stand upright (standing) with feet hip-width apart, knees soft, and core braced; hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with a neutral grip.
Maintain a slight bend in the elbows throughout and keep the torso stable (no leaning or swinging).
Initiate the movement by raising both arms out to the sides (lateral raise) until the upper arms reach about shoulder height, palms facing the floor or slightly rotated.
Pause briefly at the top of the lateral raise, then smoothly lower the weights back to your sides for a moment.
From the sides, immediately raise the arms straight forward (front raise) to shoulder height with the same slight elbow bend, keeping the motion controlled.
Pause at the top of the front raise, then slowly lower the weights back to your sides to complete one rep.
Breathe out as you lift and inhale as you lower; avoid using momentum or swinging the body—use the shoulders to move the weight.
Use a moderate weight that allows full control for the desired reps and stop if you feel shoulder pain; keep the neck relaxed and shoulders down (avoid shrugging).
exercises.description: A standing shoulder exercise that combines a lateral raise into a front raise with dumbbells to work all heads of the delts. Performed in a controlled manner to minimize momentum and emphasize shoulder activation.

exercises.name: Cable Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Set the pulleys to the lowest position and attach single D-handles; select an appropriate weight on both sides.
instructions: Set the pulleys to the lowest position and attach single D-handles; select an appropriate weight on both sides.

Stand centered between the two stations with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and a slight bend in the knees; grasp a handle in each hand with a neutral (thumb-up) grip, arms hanging down at your sides.
Cross the cables in front of you if the setup requires it so there is slight tension at the start; keep a small bend in the elbows throughout the exercise.
Brace your core and keep your torso upright (no leaning or swinging). Initiate the movement by lifting the arms out to the sides (lead with the elbows) until your hands reach about shoulder height.
Pause briefly at the top with shoulders down and back (do not shrug), then slowly lower the handles back to the start under control.
Repeat for the desired reps, exhaling as you lift and inhaling as you lower; avoid using momentum or excessive torso tilt to complete reps.
exercises.description: A standing cable lateral raise performed from low pulleys that isolates the lateral deltoid by abducting the arms out to the sides to shoulder height while keeping the torso stable. The cable setup provides constant tension through the movement for controlled concentric and eccentric phases.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Front Raise Above Head
exercises.notes: Start standing tall (neutral spine) with feet about hip-width apart, holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with a neutral grip; knees soft, core braced.
instructions: Start standing tall (neutral spine) with feet about hip-width apart, holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with a neutral grip; knees soft, core braced.
Keep a slight bend in the elbows and wrists neutral; exhale and initiate the movement by lifting the dumbbells straight in front of you using the shoulder (not the back or legs).
Continue lifting the weights along the midline until the arms are extended overhead or the dumbbells meet above the head, maintaining the small elbow bend and not locking the joints.
Pause briefly at the top while keeping the ribs down and the lower back neutral (avoid overarching); focus on shoulder (delts) engagement and scapular control.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back down in a controlled manner to the starting position at your thighs.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, avoiding swinging, using momentum, or leaning back—reduce weight if you cannot maintain strict form.
exercises.description: Standing dumbbell front raise above head is a shoulder exercise that moves the weights from your thighs up and overhead, emphasizing the anterior deltoids while recruiting chest and upper-trap stabilizers. Perform in a controlled manner to avoid momentum and lower-back compensation.

exercises.name: Cable Standing Face Pull
exercises.notes: Set a cable pulley to about eye- to upper-chest height and attach a rope handle.
instructions: Set a cable pulley to about eye- to upper-chest height and attach a rope handle.
Stand facing the machine with feet shoulder-width apart or in a slight staggered stance, knees soft and spine neutral.
Grasp the rope with both hands using a neutral/overhand grip (palms facing inward) and take a step back so there is tension on the cable.
Initiate the movement by retracting the shoulder blades and pulling the rope toward your face, driving the elbows out to the sides so your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor.
As you reach the end range, externally rotate the shoulders so the hands split apart and the rope ends are near your temples or ears; pause and squeeze the rear delts and upper back.
Slowly reverse the motion under control, extending the arms forward while maintaining scapular control until the hands return to the start position.
Repeat for the desired reps, keeping torso stable (no excessive leaning or momentum) and focusing on quality of movement and external rotation.
exercises.description: A standing cable face pull with a rope attachment that emphasizes the posterior delts and upper back by pulling the rope toward the face while externally rotating the shoulders. It develops rear shoulder strength, scapular retraction, and shoulder external rotators.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.
